local_names.romanized_local: Rave Dose (Kannada romanization); Rava Dosai (Tamil romanization)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general dosa entry (FOODON_03315595); no specific Rava Dosa entry exists. 'closest_parent' match used for semolina-based dosa variant.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pancakes and crepes — used as closest FoodEx2 analogue for griddle-cooked thin flatbread/crepe category
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No dedicated FoodEx2 code for Rava Dosa or South Indian dosa. A0FJN (Pancakes and crepes) is the closest defensible analogue in the FoodEx2 hierarchy for a thin, griddle-cooked batter product.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Rava Dosa or dosa category as of 2024.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Rava Dosa is one dosa weighing approximately 150 grams, which is a standard restaurant or home portion for a breakfast or light meal.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods is the primary approved source for Indian dishes. Rava Dosa values are adapted from ICMR-NIN semolina-based dosa and semolina (rava) composition data, cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy) for semolina and rice flour components. Saturated fat estimated from oil content typical of griddle-cooked dosa preparations. Values represent a cooked, oil-applied dosa as typically prepared.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — University of Sydney GI Database; analogous estimate for semolina-based flatbread
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for Rava Dosa exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate based on semolina-based flatbreads and refined wheat/rice flour products documented in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Diabetes Care). Semolina (durum wheat) products typically fall in the medium GI range (55–70). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (65 × 33.75g) / 100 ≈ 21.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (320 mg per 100g; ~480 mg per 150g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Rava Dosa to one serving and avoid adding extra salt to the batter; sodium from the dosa alone contributes approximately 21% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit per serving, and additional sodium from sambar or chutneys should be accounted for.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI (65) and high glycemic load per serving (~21.9) from refined semolina and rice flour
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit Rava Dosa portion size to half a serving (75g) and pair it with high-fibre coconut chutney and protein-rich sambar to blunt the postprandial glucose response, as the refined carbohydrate base (semolina and rice flour) is rapidly digested and can cause significant blood sugar elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate fat content (8.2g per 100g) with oil used for crisping; sodium load per serving
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, Rava Dosa should be prepared with minimal oil (preferably unsaturated vegetable oil) and consumed in controlled portions, as the combination of moderate fat and sodium (~480 mg per serving) can contribute to elevated blood pressure and lipid burden when consumed frequently.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Contains gluten from semolina (wheat) and maida (all-purpose flour)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis who have gluten sensitivity or wheat intolerance should avoid Rava Dosa, as the semolina and maida content may exacerbate inflammatory responses in susceptible individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Rava Dosa is a popular South Indian breakfast and snack item distinguished by its characteristic lacy, net-like texture and crispy finish. Unlike traditional fermented dosa, Rava Dosa requires no fermentation; the batter is prepared instantly from a thin, watery mixture of semolina (rava or sooji), rice flour, and all-purpose flour (maida), seasoned with cumin seeds, black peppercorns, freshly grated ginger, and chopped green chilies. The batter is poured in a circular, spreading motion onto a very hot, lightly oiled griddle (tawa), creating characteristic small holes and a delicate, crisp, net-like structure. The result is a thin, golden-brown crepe with a crunchy texture and a mildly spiced, nutty flavour from the semolina. Rava Dosa is typically served with coconut chutney and sambar (a lentil-based vegetable stew). Nutritionally, per 100g it provides approximately 180 kcal, 4.5g protein, 8.2g fat, and 22.5g carbohydrates, with 320mg sodium. The medium glycemic index (~65) and high glycemic load per serving (~21.9) make it a food that individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consume in moderation and pair with fibre and protein. It is vegetarian, vegan, and halal, but contains gluten and is not suitable for gluten-free diets. Rava Dosa is widely consumed across South India, particularly in Karnataka, Tamil Nadu, Andhra Pradesh, and Kerala, and is a staple on South Indian restaurant menus globally.
common_user_queries: Is Rava Dosa good for diabetics?
Rava Dosa vs regular dosa nutrition
meal_context: served with coconut chutney and sambar

confidence_reason: Nutritional values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (primary approved source for Indian dishes) and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for semolina and rice flour components. No single exact entry for Rava Dosa exists in ICMR-NIN; values represent a well-supported adaptation. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on semolina-based flatbread equivalents. Saturated fat is estimated from typical oil usage in griddle-cooked dosa. Confidence is medium rather than high due to the adapted (not exact) source match.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.2g per 100g) is estimated based on typical oil usage (approximately 5–8ml vegetable oil per dosa) for griddle-cooked dosa preparations; no direct saturated fat measurement for Rava Dosa was found in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Rava Dosa as a composite dish is not available in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for cooked Rava Dosa is not reported in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation. 4. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 and vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0: Rava Dosa is a plant-based food with no animal-derived ingredients; vitamin B12 is naturally zero as this is a vegan plant-based dish; vitamin D is naturally zero as no animal or fortified ingredients are present. Confirmed consistent with ICMR-NIN plant-based food data. 5. cholesterol_mg is 0.0: naturally zero as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients. 6. Portions in the input JSON contained three identical duplicate entries (all 150g); deduplicated to a single canonical serving of 150g. 7. GI of 65 is an analogous estimate; no direct Rava Dosa GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. Glycemic load per serving = (65 × 33.75) / 100 = 21.9 (based on 150g serving providing 33.75g available carbohydrate). 8. This record is for the plain Rava Dosa without fillings; masala or onion variants will have different nutritional profiles.
